author: @cmscms123456
text:
During World War 2, 'plastic' was NEW and an ounce of plastic was worth more than an ounce of GOLD, because it was new, and so hard to produce.  Today, we make trash bags out of plastics.
Today, graphene is NEW and VERY EXPENSIVE to produce... sound familiar?  By 2025, GRAPHENE SOLAR, GRAPHENE BATTERIES, GRAPHENE SUPER CONDUCTORS...  ALL ARE COMING SOON.
BIG LOSERS WILL BE fossil fuels, BIG OIL, natural gas, lithium batteries<< HAHA  JOKE BATTERIES...    A Graphene battery has been 3D printed with 10 times the capacity in the same size/area as a current lithium battery.  By 2030, you wont be able to find a "GAS STATION"..  so take a picture of one now.

author: @DeliciousDeBlair
text:
So how many layers, or, how thick of a single layer can you make with this method, and experience increasing gains?

Would that be micrometers, millimeters or more possibly?

Since in theory roughly 1100 watts of sunlight [this is only an average I realize, and the nearer the pole or the equator one gets it goes higher or lower] hits the earth at midday, what percentage of a given wattage of sunlight is theoretically possible to be trapped and converted to current before the laws of diminishing returns says its too thick, too bulky, too expensive to build, etc.

Is thicker better even?

Also, does one want to make this in thin strips for total combined voltage and to prevent self neutralization, or does one want to make wide sheets in hopes of greater surface utilization, is the terminal voltage more useful than the terminal current, etc?

author: @richpalmisano1740
text:
Ok, my idea spurned from this would be instead of painting this onto a sheet, leave it in it's liquid form (as you demonstrated that it's reactive with a light) and add the graphene to the original solution.  It might need a "stirrer" of some sort to allow the graphene to circulate in the tube, but easy enough.

This could be done in a capped glass tube with a copper/aluminum annode and cathode inside the tube.  Add a parabolic mirror behind the glass tube and viola!  It appears that if you have more 'density' of material you will generate more amperage.

author: @bradmiller7001
text:
I just love the kid in the sweet shop, mate!  As I am watching this video - I see a very transparent single sheet that is letting a massive amount of light move right through the material.  This leads me too two key questions, how many sheets can be layered together to where we get 50% light reduction. If we were to place a small mirror on the back we would be getting nearly 85% of all the light absorbed by the material.   The point is this, if the cost is low and with low heat gain, this is very significant. 

Each sheet should have some air space between layers and this gives a significant advantage to your design.   I would suggest the reflective material used by the hydroponic growing crowd as the back stop, Reflective Mylar.    It comes down to cost per watt but I think you may have over looked something here. 

How about a revisit -  using 8.5 x 11 inch standard plastic sheets, can you produce more than a single watt output, using multiple layers  -  The magic dollar cost is 0.48 Euro Dollars per watt as a production cost.   So the home production cost should be about $48.00 Euros a watt as a goal.  

If that can be done, then the revolution has started!   You have the resources by using conductive inks for the copper and aluminum strips (more plastic cut and bonded into place).  What I think, I am seeing is printed multi layered solar cells.   Back in my High School days I ran an old printing press, much like the one I have seen in your new building.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: +Brad Miller I think you are right mate - i have over looked this - the stacking is a great idea - time for a revisit definately

replies.author: @bradmiller7001
replies.text: Thanks for the positive reply.. I look forward to the results.. and the next video.

replies.text: +Brad Miller i always appreciate good thinking mate - and i think you are spot on there - cheers

text: Have you been successful with fabricating single layer graphene oxide sheets? author: @BrewmasterJack
text: This is all very exciting and the video has me itching to try it out!  In falling down the rabbit hole of your YouTube channel I came across a video about using a water/acetone mixture as a solvent for liquid phase exfoliation  of graphene.  I'm just curious if this is the method you used to produce the graphene used in this experiment.  In doing some research I found a source saying that acetone can react violently with acetic acid.  If the nano cellulose was produced from bacteria, I suppose there's a chance that some acetic acid could have been produced.  This is coming from a background in brewing, not chemistry.  So I could be way off!  Any thoughts you have would be much appreciated.  Thank you very much for posting these videos!  Keep up the great work!

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: +Tyler Guilmette i used a different method to make the graphene mate - i am glad you like the vid too

author: @gristlevonraben
text: I wonder if you put it on in layers, instead of in a block.  A block allows for the whole to act as a big collector, but layers act as capacitor plates allowing charges to resonate between the plates, thereby sometimes enhancing signal strength, as you know, or a charge.  I guess the question would be whether or not the plates would form negative and positive reversals under each piece.

author: @IAMainvision
text: so, who was the clever sod who rediscovered 'Graphene'? (single layer carbon surfacing). Great videos! I have simpler way to create industrial 'grade' graphene. Use the principles of electrostatic painting on acetate film with a paper stencil... or simply print your schematics (film areas) on acetate (transparency film) with your mono laser printer and electroplate it afterwards (if necessary)... That's how I do it.

author: @misteragreeable8360
text: I was thinking along the lines of Gandt, but it occurs to me that there may be an opportunity for testing not mentioned yet. A wet cell. As simple as the solution in a test tube with the anode and cathode running down the side. With a large numbers of electrons jumping around in  the solution, there'd be a great chance for them be harvested. Especially if the solution was highly conductive with  graphene solution. A perfect chance to use the much easier to make graphene in solution. A little tea, a little zinc, and presto.

Considering its hardly damping the light that passes throgh, and the size of the cell. I'd say thats fairly promising.

You could slack a few up before there was no light reaching the bottom of the stack, or make solar windows, or reflect the light back through as others have sugested. Solar greenhouse even....

I wonder if it would go better if you formed a semiconductive oxide layer on one or both of the metal strips. Could raise the short circuit voltage a bit if nothing else. Did you happen to note down the Short circuit voltages with a 10m+ impedance meter (like the one your using in this video)?

author: @rotr9336
text: Im thoroughly confused by this! :-)   Do you still think this is a MIM diode spread out over the whole piece of plastic? Whats really puzzling me is that the mixture you put on there shouldnt really have an orientation, right? So any current that is generated in one particle would just cancel out some current generated in another particle, because theyre all just randomly connected up by the graphene, before even reaching the electrodes... Maybe then only the areas close to the electrodes contribute to what you measure? Could perhaps also explain why your results in the previous video are better.. What do you think?

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: i think the graphene is conducting it away quickly enough - or you are right - either way - it is interesting and probably deserves looking at

author: @FredGandt
text:
*Flexible transparent solar cells - on the cheap!*
I realise that "flexible" is good, but wouldn't the "transparent" part lend itself nicely to being applied to windows?
I'm sure I've not seen anyone suggest it yet, which surprises me.
A reasonably long lasting compound that could be painted onto the inside of greenhouses could be awesome, especially if adopted by industrial farmers of _e.g._ tomatoes.
Then being flexible might come back into the useful zone, since polytunnels could also be _treated._

You're a real powerhouse of innovation Rob.

author: @joeglory425
text: Fascinating! How would you go about boosting the voltage? For tripling the voltage, could it be as simple as placing parallel strips of copper, aluminum, copper, aluminum, copper, aluminum all on the same sheet and then painting over it with magic paint? The inner strips of aluminum and copper being connected to nothing while the outermost strips are hooked up to the voltmeter/load.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: you'd want to increase the amps mate - increasing volts would just be a matter of having a few wired in series. You would increase the amps by having more active material accessible to the light, so - put more in - make it thicker - something like that

replies.author: @joeglory425
replies.text: Robert Murray-Smith Oh definitely, the amps have to be increased; but so does the voltage.  A silicon solar cell only puts out about 0.7 volts, so they're wired in series.  I've "tabbed" solar cells in series before and I didn't like doing it.  I broke quite a few in the process.  I was thinking you could bypass the whole process of tabbing cells together in series, provided the layout I suggested actually does raise voltage (It might not!).  It would make building solar panels so much easier.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text:
Joseph Richardson
sorry mate but an electrochemical process is going to be limited by the reaction voltage which is a combination of the redox half voltages and influenced by the electrolyte. A solar cell fits into this schema - so other strategies have to be used to increase voltage

replies.author: @joeglory425
replies.text: I don't really understand your response.  Please elaborate.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text:
Joseph Richardson
physical processes have limits. It's like trying to nail wood together if you have two boards at 2 inch each it is no good trying to fix them with a two inch nail however many two inch nails you have in there they just won't fix together - what you need is a 4 inch nail. It's the same here - the process behind the conversion has a limit - it won't help making it thicker or putting more stuff in there - using more nails - it won't go beyond the limit - you need to change the process - get a bigger nail - to get more out of it - of course there is a limit to that too - ever tried to fix a bit of 1/8th inch trim with a 6 inch nail? - hope this helped
